message:
fix(stdio/file): survive overflow and alloc failure in MemBacking::Dynamic::write

`MemBacking::Dynamic::write(src)` computed the new tail as
`*pos + src.len()`. On a stream seeked near `i64::MAX` / `usize::MAX`
this wraps and the subsequent `data.resize(end, 0)` either attempts
a smaller allocation (silently corrupting positions) or aborts the
process on platforms where the allocator panics on oversized
requests. Either way, a fwrite on a dynamic memstream that had been
`fseek`ed to a huge offset could crash a perfectly well-formed
caller.

This change:

- Replaces `let end = *pos + src.len();` with a
  `let Some(end) = pos.checked_add(src.len()) else { return 0; };`
  so overflow is treated as a "wrote zero bytes" signal that the
  glibc fwrite wrapper already understands; no panic, no UB.
- When the write does need to extend the buffer, uses
  `data.try_reserve(end - data.len())` before `data.resize(end, 0)`,
  so an out-of-memory condition also returns 0 instead of aborting.
  The caller sees a short-write and can set ferror; on glibc this is
  the same visible behaviour as fwrite to a kernel-backed stream
  under ENOMEM.
- Adds `test_mem_backing_dynamic_huge_seek_write_fails_without_panic`:
  seeks the stream to `i64::MAX`, writes one byte, asserts the write
  returned 0, the data buffer stayed empty, and the stream position
  is still the requested offset (we don't implicitly rewind).

No public API change. Closes the panic path that fuzz_stdio's
seek+write corpus had been tripping on intermittently.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
